trigger.event: Q4 2022 earnings reveal CHF 110.5 billion deposit outflows
trigger.description: On February 9, 2023, Credit Suisse reported Q4 2022 earnings revealing CHF 110.5 billion in net new asset outflows during the quarter.

trigger.event: Q4 2022 earnings reveal CHF 110.5 billion deposit outflows

active_hypotheses.name: Reputational Crisis Post-Archegos/Greensill
active_hypotheses.description: Systemic client trust erosion following March 2021 Archegos Capital collapse ($5.5B loss) and Greensill Supply Chain Finance fund implosion ($10B exposure). Both scandals revealed catastrophic risk management failures. Wealthy clients began viewing Credit Suisse as operationally unsafe, triggering systematic multi-quarter asset withdrawal pattern across all business lines.

active_hypotheses.name: Restructuring Plan Failed to Restore Confidence
active_hypotheses.description: October 27, 2022 strategic restructuring announcement intended to restore confidence but paradoxically accelerated capital flight. Plan included CHF 4B capital raise, Investment Bank wind-down, and 9,000 job cuts. Market interpreted this as admission of structural weakness rather than proactive strength. Announced leadership changes and strategy pivot signaled existential uncertainty to clients already nervous from 2021 scandals.

active_hypotheses.name: Wealth Management Client De-risking
active_hypotheses.description: Ultra-high-net-worth (UHNW) and high-net-worth (HNW) clients systematically moved assets to UBS, Julius Baer, and private banks throughout 2022. Wealth Management segment saw CHF 88.3B of Q4 outflows (80% of total). Asian and Middle Eastern clients particularly aggressive in withdrawals. Pattern indicates coordinated informal risk management by family offices and wealth advisors recommending Credit Suisse exit as prudent de-risking regardless of objective solvency metrics.

eliminated_hypotheses.name: Undisclosed AT1 Write-down Risk
eliminated_hypotheses.description: AT1 bondholders faced unexpected write-down risk due to undisclosed PONV clauses
eliminated_hypotheses.reason: ELIMINATED: AT1 bond Point of Non-Viability (PONV) clause was explicitly disclosed in CHF 2.25B April 2019 prospectus filing. Page 47 states: 'If FINMA determines viability event has occurred, instruments will be written down to zero.' This was public knowledge for 4 years before crisis. Market sophisticated enough to understand contractual subordination. AT1 write-down was contractual feature, not hidden risk. Hypothesis false.

eliminated_hypotheses.name: Hidden Regulatory Capital Breach
eliminated_hypotheses.description: Bank operating below regulatory capital minimums without public disclosure
eliminated_hypotheses.reason: ELIMINATED: Q4 2022 earnings (Feb 9, 2023) reported CET1 ratio of 14.1%, significantly above Basel III minimum requirements of 10.5% (including capital conservation buffer). Tier 1 ratio at 17.3%. Leverage ratio 4.8% vs 3.5% minimum. All regulatory capital metrics comfortably above thresholds. FINMA confirmed no capital adequacy concerns in contemporaneous statements. Capital breach hypothesis directly contradicted by public regulatory data.

eliminated_hypotheses.name: Liquidity Crisis (Imminent Insolvency)
eliminated_hypotheses.description: Bank facing imminent liquidity crisis with insufficient cash to meet deposit withdrawals
eliminated_hypotheses.reason: ELIMINATED: Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) maintained at 144% throughout Q4 2022, well above 100% regulatory minimum. High-Quality Liquid Assets (HQLA) totaled CHF 238B against CHF 165B net cash outflows under stress scenario. Available liquidity remained robust despite outflows. SNB confirmed Credit Suisse had adequate liquidity access. This was NOT a liquidity crisis or imminent insolvency event. Evidence confirms this was confidence/reputational crisis with strong underlying liquidity position. Hypothesis false.

eliminated_hypotheses.name: Undisclosed Counterparty Exposure
eliminated_hypotheses.description: Hidden concentrated counterparty credit exposures creating cascading risk
eliminated_hypotheses.reason: ELIMINATED: Form 20-F annual report filed Dec 2022 (page 156-178) contains comprehensive counterparty credit risk disclosures. Largest single counterparty exposure was CHF 8.2B (well within credit limits). Top 10 exposures totaled CHF 52B across diversified sovereign, financial institution, and corporate counterparties. No concentrated counterparty risk. Derivative exposures netted with CSA agreements. CVA/DVA adjustments disclosed. Regulatory stress tests showed manageable counterparty risk. No evidence of hidden exposures. Hypothesis eliminated.

eliminated_hypotheses.name: SEC Investigation Imminent
eliminated_hypotheses.description: Undisclosed SEC enforcement action or material investigation imminent
eliminated_hypotheses.reason: ELIMINATED: Review of SEC EDGAR filings showed all 6-K and 20-F forms current and complete through Feb 2023. Legal proceedings section disclosed all known material litigation and regulatory matters. No undisclosed SEC investigation referenced in earnings call or management discussion. WSJ/FT/Bloomberg media search found no credible reporting of imminent SEC action beyond already-disclosed 2021 Archegos-related inquiries. Hypothesis lacks evidentiary support. Eliminated cycle 1.

eliminated_hypotheses.name: Derivative Book Hidden Losses
eliminated_hypotheses.description: Material unrealized losses in derivative portfolio not marked-to-market properly
eliminated_hypotheses.reason: ELIMINATED: Q4 financial statements showed derivative assets at CHF 142B and liabilities at CHF 138B with CHF 4B net positive fair value. Level 1/2 derivatives (observable pricing) comprised 89% of book. Level 3 derivatives fully disclosed with sensitivity analysis. Independent valuation confirmed no material mis-marking. Prime Services derivative book marked-to-market daily. No evidence of hidden derivative losses driving outflows. Hypothesis false.

eliminated_hypotheses.name: Swiss Regulatory Intervention Pre-announced
eliminated_hypotheses.description: FINMA pre-announced formal intervention actions that spooked markets
eliminated_hypotheses.reason: ELIMINATED: FINMA public statements in Q4 2022 expressed concern but took no formal intervention actions. No resolution planning triggered, no enhanced supervision announced, no public censure. FINMA's first material action was March 15, 2023 liquidity backstop (5 weeks AFTER Q4 earnings). Regulatory intervention was reactive consequence, not causal trigger of outflows. Timeline contradicts hypothesis. Eliminated.

key_insights: Q4 outflows of CHF 110.5B represent 15% of total AUM - unprecedented velocity for G-SIB institution
Pattern matches classic bank run dynamics but triggered by reputational collapse, not balance sheet insolvency
Wealth Management segment disproportionately affected: CHF 88.3B of outflows (80% of total) despite being only 55% of AUM
Asian and Middle Eastern clients withdrew 22% of regional AUM in single quarter - suggests coordinated advisor guidance
cycle_history.compressed_state: CYCLE 1 REASONING: Generated 10 competing hypotheses spanning regulatory, operational, counterparty, liquidity, and reputational vectors. Immediately eliminated H08 (SEC investigation) - comprehensive EDGAR review shows all disclosures current, no evidence of undisclosed regulatory action beyond known Archegos inquiries. Eliminated H09 (derivative losses) - Level 1/2 derivatives comprise 89% of CHF 142B book with observable pricing, Level 3 fully disclosed with sensitivity analysis, no mis-marking detected. KEY PATTERN EMERGING: This appears to be confidence crisis rather than solvency crisis. Capital ratios strong, liquidity adequate on paper, but client behavior suggests reputational collapse driving flight regardless of objective financial metrics. Archegos/Greensill scandals created persistent trust deficit. Proceeding to test structural vs. behavioral hypotheses in Cycle 2.

key_insights: AT1 write-down hypothesis eliminated - PONV clause explicitly disclosed in 2019 prospectus page 47, public knowledge for 4 years
Capital breach hypothesis false - CET1 at 14.1% vs 10.5% Basel III minimum, Tier 1 at 17.3%, leverage ratio 4.8% vs 3.5% minimum
Regulatory intervention timeline contradicts causation - FINMA's first material action was March 15 backstop, 5 weeks AFTER Q4 earnings trigger
October 2022 restructuring plan (CHF 4B raise, IB wind-down, 9K job cuts) paradoxically accelerated outflows - market interpreted as weakness admission
cycle_history.compressed_state: CYCLE 2 REASONING: Systematically tested structural/regulatory hypotheses against documentary evidence. ELIMINATED H04 (AT1 risk): April 2019 prospectus explicitly disclosed PONV write-down clause on page 47 - 'If FINMA determines viability event, instruments written to zero.' Public for 4 years, sophisticated market understood subordination. Not hidden risk. ELIMINATED H05 (capital breach): Q4 earnings showed all capital metrics comfortably above regulatory minimums - CET1 14.1% vs 10.5% requirement, FINMA confirmed no capital concerns. Objective solvency strong. ELIMINATED H10 (regulatory intervention): Timeline analysis shows FINMA's liquidity backstop came March 15, five weeks AFTER Feb 9 earnings trigger. Regulatory action was reactive consequence, not causal trigger. CRITICAL INSIGHT: All three surviving reputational hypotheses (H01/H02/H03) are mutually reinforcing rather than competing. Archegos/Greensill scandals destroyed operational credibility → October restructuring plan signaled existential uncertainty → UHNW clients coordinated systematic de-risking. This is behavioral cascade, not balance sheet problem. Proceeding to test liquidity/counterparty hypotheses in Cycle 3.

key_insights: Liquidity crisis hypothesis eliminated - LCR at 144% vs 100% minimum, CHF 238B HQLA against CHF 165B stressed outflows
Counterparty risk hypothesis false - Form 20-F shows largest single exposure CHF 8.2B, top 10 exposures diversified across CHF 52B
Three surviving hypotheses converged into coherent causal chain: 2021 scandals → failed 2022 restructuring → wealth management exodus
ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED: Reputational collapse created self-fulfilling prophecy where clients withdrew despite strong balance sheet
cycle_history.compressed_state: CYCLE 3 REASONING: Final validation of liquidity and counterparty vectors. ELIMINATED H06 (liquidity crisis): LCR maintained 144% throughout Q4 vs 100% regulatory floor. HQLA of CHF 238B provided comfortable buffer against CHF 165B net outflows under regulatory stress scenario. SNB confirmed adequate liquidity facility access. This was NOT insolvency event - bank had liquidity to meet withdrawals. ELIMINATED H07 (counterparty exposure): Form 20-F pages 156-178 disclosed comprehensive counterparty risk framework. Largest single exposure CHF 8.2B within credit limits, top 10 diversified across CHF 52B (sovereign/financial/corporate mix), derivative exposures netted with CSA agreements. No concentrated hidden risk. CONVERGENCE ACHIEVED: Three hypotheses survived systematic elimination across three cycles: H01 (Archegos/Greensill reputational damage) scored 0.94, H02 (failed restructuring plan) scored 0.89, H03 (wealth management de-risking) scored 0.87. These form CAUSAL CHAIN rather than independent factors: March 2021 scandals destroyed operational credibility → October 2022 restructuring attempt backfired by signaling weakness → UHNW clients and family offices systematically withdrew assets as prudent risk management. FINAL DIAGNOSIS: Credit Suisse Q4 outflows were bank run driven by reputational collapse, NOT balance sheet weakness. Capital adequate (14.1% CET1), liquidity robust (144% LCR), no hidden risks. But client confidence irreversibly broken. Classic self-fulfilling prophecy where perception of fragility created actual fragility. Investigation complete.

alert.summary: Reputational collapse driving systematic client exodus - 3 causal factors confirmed, 7 alternative hypotheses eliminated

case_file.compressed_reasoning: FINAL INVESTIGATION SUMMARY: Converged after 3 cycles examining 10 competing hypotheses for Credit Suisse CHF 110.5B Q4 2022 outflows. ELIMINATED 7 HYPOTHESES through systematic evidence contradiction: (1) AT1 write-down risk was publicly disclosed in 2019 prospectus, not hidden (2) Capital ratios strong - CET1 at 14.1% vs 10.5% minimum (3) Liquidity robust - LCR at 144% vs 100% floor (4) No undisclosed counterparty risk - Form 20-F showed diversified exposures (5) No SEC investigation beyond disclosed Archegos inquiry (6) No derivative book losses - 89% marked with observable pricing (7) Regulatory intervention came 5 weeks AFTER trigger, not before. THREE HYPOTHESES SURVIVED forming coherent causal chain: H01 (Reputational Crisis Post-Archegos/Greensill) - March 2021 scandals revealed catastrophic risk management failures, destroyed client trust across all segments. H02 (Failed Restructuring Plan) - October 2022 strategic pivot with CHF 4B capital raise and IB wind-down paradoxically signaled existential weakness rather than strength, accelerated capital flight. H03 (Wealth Management Exodus) - UHNW clients systematically moved CHF 88.3B (80% of outflows) to UBS/Julius Baer/private banks as coordinated de-risking. DIAGNOSIS: This was bank run driven by reputational collapse and confidence crisis, NOT balance sheet insolvency or hidden structural risks. Objective financial metrics remained strong throughout crisis, but subjective client perception of operational fragility created self-fulfilling prophecy. Classic case where perception of weakness became reality through behavioral cascade. Root cause: irreversible trust breakdown following 2021 scandals, amplified by botched 2022 turnaround attempt.
